text: <par>Lahu <latinform>ní</latinform> could alternatively descend from the <latinform>*s-nip</latinform> variant, since the Lahu reflex of <latinform>*-ip</latinform> is <latinform>í</latinform> after *glottalized initials.</par>
<par>This Jingpho form appears to be a loan from Burmese, since native etyma with final <reconstruction>*-k</reconstruction> acquire Jingpho <latinform>-ʔ</latinform>.</par>
<par>Final nasal in Jingpho <latinform>-ūm</latinform> perhaps result of assimilation to preceding round vowel.</par>
<par>For a list of some thirty Black Lahu names for particular species of mushrooms, see DL:1004-1005.</par>
<par>The second syllable is a borrowing from Indic (cf. Myanmar-English Dictionary), cf. Sanskrit siṁhá.</par>
<par>This record has no reflex; I was not able to locate a form in the source.</par>
